full_response:
We work in Schwarzschild coordinates  
x^μ = (t , r , θ , φ) with line element  

ds² = g_{μν} dx^μ dx^ν  
   = −(1 − 2GM/r) dt² + (1 − 2GM/r)^{-1} dr² + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).

The curve is specified by  
r = r₀ (constant > 2GM), θ = θ₀ (constant), φ = φ₀ (constant),  
so the only varying coordinate is t.  
Its (coordinate–basis) tangent 4-vector is therefore  

U^μ = (dt/dλ , dr/dλ , dθ/dλ , dφ/dλ) = (1, 0, 0, 0).

Step 1:  Compute the norm U·U = g_{μν} U^μ U^ν.

Because only the t–component is non–zero,

U·U = g_{tt} (U^t)² = g_{tt} = −(1 − 2GM/r₀).

Step 2:  Evaluate the sign for r₀ > 2GM.

For r₀ > 2GM  ⇒  (1 − 2GM/r₀) > 0,  
so

U·U = −(positive number) < 0.

With the “mostly plus” signature (−, +, +, +) used here,
• negative norm  ⇒  timelike,  
• zero norm      ⇒  null,  
• positive norm  ⇒  spacelike.

Thus the given tangent vector is timelike (though not unit-normalized; a physical 4-velocity would be Û^μ = (1/√(1−2GM/r₀), 0,0,0)).

\boxed{U\cdot U = -\left(1-\dfrac{2GM}{r_0}\right) < 0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{timelike for } r_0>2GM}

Confidence: 92%
